:::{#lem-surv-discretized}
#### Exact discretization of the survival factor

Partition $[0, \max_j Y_j]$ into intervals of width $\Delta t$ with left endpoints $\tau$,
and assume each observed time $Y_j$ falls on an interval boundary
(for an interior $Y_j$ the identity instead holds in the $\Delta t \to 0$ limit).
Then each subject's survival factor is the *exact* product
of its per-interval conditional survival probabilities:

$$
\surv(Y_j \mid \vx_j) = \prod_{\tau < Y_j} \paren{1 - q_j(\tau)},
$$ {#eq-surv-discretized}

where $q_j(\tau)$ is the interval failure probability of @def-interval-fail-prob.
:::

{{< slidebreak >}}

::: {.proof}
Surviving to $Y_j$ is the intersection of the events "survive interval $\tau$",
over all grid intervals $\tau < Y_j$,
which tile $[0, Y_j)$ exactly because $Y_j$ is a grid boundary.
By the chain rule for survival over a partition,
the probability of this intersection factors into the product,
over those intervals, of the conditional probability of surviving each interval
given survival to its left endpoint.
That conditional survival probability is, by definition, $1 - q_j(\tau)$.
The factorization is *exact*: each factor is the exact conditional survival probability
$1 - q_j(\tau)$, not its first-order hazard approximation,
so no error is incurred at this step.
:::

:::{#exm-surv-discretized}
#### Discretizing a constant hazard

Suppose subject $j$ has constant hazard $\haz(t \mid \vx_j) = 0.1$/yr
and is observed to $Y_j = 1.5$ yr,
discretized with $\Delta t = 0.5$ yr (three intervals, $\tau = 0,\, 0.5,\, 1.0$).
Each exact per-interval survival factor is

$$
\ba
1 - q_j(\tau)
&= \expf{-\textstyle\int_{\tau}^{\tau + 0.5} 0.1 \, du}
&& \text{(exact conditional survival over the interval)}
\\
&= \expf{-0.05}
&& \text{(evaluate the integral: } 0.1 \times 0.5\text{)}
\\
&\approx 0.9512 ,
&& \text{(arithmetic)}
\ea
$$

so @eq-surv-discretized gives

$$
\ba
\surv(1.5 \mid \vx_j)
&= \prod_{\tau \in \set{0,\, 0.5,\, 1.0}} \paren{1 - q_j(\tau)}
&& \text{(@eq-surv-discretized, three intervals)}
\\
&\approx 0.9512^3
&& \text{(substitute the common per-interval factor)}
\\
&\approx 0.8607 ,
&& \text{(arithmetic)}
\ea
$$

which matches the closed form $\surv(1.5 \mid \vx_j) = \expf{-0.1 \times 1.5} = \expf{-0.15} \approx 0.8607$ exactly,
confirming that the discretization introduces no error.
:::
